segments.title: Payroll Modernization Overview -- AS400 Manual Processes, Employee Self-Service Vision, and HCM System Architecture
segments.summary: Finance Committee convenes long-awaited payroll modernization presentation. Josh Syrson (API project manager), CFO, and Mike Smith (Assistant Director HR) present objectives: (1) improved business processes -- current employee status changes require printing AS400 screenshot, handwriting changes, scanning, DocuSigning (10-15 min per change vs seconds in modern system); employee self-service for address updates, open enrollment, direct deposit; (2) sustainable payroll management -- weekly + bi-weekly + SURA payroll cycles, paper time sheets from 2,000-person organization, 2 retirees + 3 staff in payroll, recently processed 24 police officer retropay corrections dating to 2021 (40 staff hours for 24 corrections); (3) workforce analytics -- overtime cost analysis currently impossible, compliance tracking across departments, data-driven scheduling. System diagram: timekeeping (SWC/TeleStaff) and HCM (HR + payroll) shown as cyclical process; AS400 handles demographic data and payroll but lacks position management, applicant tracking, employee self-service. Current demographic data described as 'incomplete at best' and 'isolated' -- cannot generate reports or track work authorization status. Nine union contracts with complex deduction cascades make every personnel change multi-step.
processing_notes: HR, Fire, Finance, IT, API in detected_departments are all contextually relevant; API, HR, Finance are presenting departments
TeleStaff = public safety scheduling software being used for payroll (not its intended purpose)
'Secondary jobs' = AS400 term for contractual add-ons (uniform allowance, sick time buyback, over-the-cap bonus)
SURA = Syracuse Urban Renewal Agency (separate payroll cycle)
CFO first name 'Mike' mentioned but last name not stated in transcript

segments.title: SWC Rollout at 70% Live, Oracle Failure Analysis, UKG Pro Selection, and Mosaic Consulting RFP
segments.summary: SWC digital timekeeping at 70% live across city (TeleStaff for public safety, SWC for civilian departments). Water fully live, Parks near-complete; additional portion 'ready' but phased to ensure support bandwidth (~250 employees max per month go-live). Ehrenreich asks why standard payroll (Paychex/ADP) wasn't used -- ADP evaluated but per-employee costs reached tens of thousands monthly; checks would mail from Cleveland (employees want same-day local pickup). Oracle Fusion analysis: previous contractor claimed Oracle would integrate with TeleStaff/SWC but 'that wasn't the case' -- CFO compares to 'Android phone with iPhone charger.' Syracuse City School District adding $900K in salaries to get Oracle operational; city would have followed same path. Re-evaluated: UKG Pro selected as SaaS ('leasing a car vs building a car') -- vendor handles maintenance/updates, competitive price, same ecosystem as existing SWC/TeleStaff. RFP issued for implementation services; Mosaic Consulting Group selected as 'most qualified by far.' If Oracle had gone live a year ago, it would have covered all HCM modules shown in green on diagram; without it, city stranded in timekeeping-only mode. Rolling out SWC without HCM foundation causes ongoing pain: new hires delayed weeks in system, PTO balances out of date, org chart not maintained.

political_signal.description: Council questioning why standard payroll processors weren't used and pressing for cost justification vs commercial alternatives

processing_notes: HR and Parks in detected_departments are contextual (Parks = department rollout example); presenting departments are API, HR, Finance
$5,000 = example salary increase amount; $10,000 = erroneous water worker paycheck (recouped)
Oracle Fusion = previously selected HCM that was abandoned before go-live
$900K = Syracuse City School District additional salary costs for Oracle implementation
ADP, Paychex = commercial payroll processors evaluated and rejected

segments.title: SWC Challenges Without HCM, Bi-Weekly Payroll Fix, Oracle Decision, Staffing, and 'Why Is It Different Now?'
segments.summary: Ongoing SWC challenges persist without HCM: aging software requires multiple license types (clock, computer, payroll each separate); no mobile punch capability; county server dependency adds failure points; new hires delayed weeks in SWC; PTO balances out of date; org chart not maintained. 2025 accomplishment: bi-weekly payroll period shifted from Wednesday-Tuesday to Sunday-Saturday, enabling accurate SWC time card approval (previously employees approved time before working final days). Business process mapping completed. UKG Pro selected over Oracle; Mosaic chosen via competitive RFP. ~10 employees assigned (5-6 full-time dedicated); may need 1-2 additional hires. Time savings from automation expected to offset implementation staffing. Finance Committee Chair directly asks 'why is it different now?' after two years of the same presentation. Mike Smith responds: better team structure reducing HR/API silos; more active leadership engagement; thorough business process understanding; correct software (UKG over Oracle); right partner (Mosaic via competitive RFP vs prior waiver-of-competitive-bid contracts). Smith declares passionately: 'We're sick of it. We are so tired of dealing with AS400 and processing paperwork like we're still running the Erie Canal. We're a modern city... and a major part of our office equipment is a pen. We're done. We're over it.'

political_signal.description: Finance Committee Chair directly asks 'why is it different now?' after years and millions spent on payroll modernization; simultaneous championship from staff passionately advocating for change

segments.title: SWC Culture Change, Module Prioritization If Underfunded, County Mosaic Alignment, and $7.8M Project Cost to Date
segments.summary: SWC rollout driving culture change -- time sheets historically 'felt optional' with some employees not submitting for months; DocuSign delays led to paying employees after separation. SWC end of life March 2027; parallel rollout continues (timekeeping + HCM design simultaneously). TeleStaff confirmed as scheduling software repurposed for payroll; UKG Pro will properly connect scheduling with accrual management. Onondaga County also evaluating Mosaic for Kronos-to-cloud migration. Councilor Moore (experienced with Dayforce from prior NYS employment) asks about module prioritization if funding cut to 50% -- musts: demographic data, payroll, timekeeping, position management; employee self-service debated (staff argues essential for direct deposit phishing prevention, W-2 distribution, training tracking); performance management first to cut; AI chatbot module deliberately excluded from scope. Council asks total spend: $7.8M over ~5.5 years (2020-2025), roughly half SWC and half Oracle. Contractor cleanup: EY relationship ended late 2024; ERP1, Dear Alien Bait, Cherro Technologies eliminated. Active: UKG (Kronos license), Kathleen LLC (former AS400 programmer), Central City Data (former API employee doing AS400 programming).

political_signal.description: Council demanding total cost accounting after $7.8M spent and asking about module prioritization if full funding not approved

segments.title: Staffing Impact, Applicant Tracking Gap, Concierge Onboarding, AS400 Sunset Timeline, and Regional Systems Landscape
segments.summary: Staff deployment question: new system won't reduce headcount but redirects effort from manual compilation to value-added analytics (e.g., analyzing water department overtime spikes vs just processing paychecks). Current applicant tracking entirely paper-based; city lost Indeed full suite access -- limited to 3 job postings/month, no automated applicant communications. UKG applicant tracking module provides centralized database, automated emails to applicants, concierge onboarding (pre-hire paperwork, integrated training, E-Verify). AS400 sunset targets: timekeeping decommissioned July 1, 2026 (successful SWC rollout); payroll/HR demographics July 1, 2027 (12-18 month UKG Pro implementation). AS400 also used for water billing and assessment -- those remain. County landscape: PeopleSoft for general ledger/AP/AR (city piggybacks); county invested heavily in PeopleSoft project costing. City grant cost allocation currently 100% manual journal entries -- modern system would automate grant reimbursement tracking (e.g., police overtime auto-allocated to grant). Rochester school district spent $30M+ on Oracle. No New York municipality currently live on UKG Pro; State of Indiana and Boulder, Colorado cited. Council asks about city peer models.

political_signal.description: Council pressing on AS400 sunset timeline and asking for peer city models as validation

processing_notes: HR and IT in detected_departments are contextually correct; API, HR, Finance are presenting departments
Indeed = job posting platform (city lost full suite access -- significant hiring disadvantage)
E-Verify = federal employment eligibility verification; PeopleSoft = Oracle's older HR/ERP used by county
Rochester school district $30M+ Oracle implementation = regional cautionary tale
July 1 2026 AS400 timekeeping sunset and July 1 2027 payroll sunset = key milestones to track

segments.title: $7.8M Project Spend Breakdown, $15M Police/Fire Overtime Without Reports, Contractor History, and SWC Water Success
segments.summary: Total project spend confirmed at $7.8M over ~5.5 years (2020-2025), split roughly half SWC and half Oracle. Costs ballooned in 2024 due to EY consulting. Contractor cleanup detailed: EY, ERP1, Dear Alien Bait, Cherro Technologies all terminated; active contracts: UKG (Kronos license), Kathleen LLC (former AS400 programmer), Central City Data (former API employee). CFO highlights systemic gap: $330M+ operating budget but unable to run on-demand overtime listing; police and fire departments spending nearly $15M in overtime without decipherable analytical reports. SWC Water Department success story: 5 months live, department reports fewer paycheck errors, employees now have overtime hour visibility before paychecks arrive. Culture change trajectory: initial resistance gives way to compliance once benefits demonstrated; Water first to admit system is working and saving time. County also evaluating Mosaic for cloud migration. Council member asks about department head resistance to digital transition -- confirmed as real challenge but improving with demonstrated results. Training on company time, not personal time.

political_signal.description: Council pressing for total dollar figures; CFO's revelation of $15M police/fire overtime without analytical reports highlights governance gap

segments.title: Workday Evaluation, Training Plan, Council Expenditure Hesitancy, July 2027 Timeline, and Project Ownership Group
segments.summary: Workday evaluated but rejected -- would require scrapping SWC timekeeping and retraining on entirely new platform. Training plan: employee-facing website with video courses and self-paced modules; managerial role-specific training; help desk same-day response for live departments; office hours sessions. SWC training added to formal onboarding alongside sexual harassment training. Finance Committee Chair explicitly states: 'I hope you also understand why the council is hesitant to approve another major expenditure for a project that seems like we've been asked to do so a number of times already.' Without Mosaic, UKG Pro rollout would take 3-5 years (no internal UKG Pro expertise) vs ~1 year with partner. Project asks: ~$1M implementation services + ~$750K recurring annual. Josh Syrson adds to 'what's different' answer: past had too many vendors producing disjointed pieces; now extensive internal process mapping done, central API leadership vs prior siloed approach where 'consultants preyed on' split between HR and payroll. Digitizing bad processes doesn't make them better -- examined processes first. Project ownership group: Deputy Mayor, API Director, HR Director, CFO, First Deputy Commissioner of Finance. Expected completion: June/July 2027. Fully cloud-based system. Mosaic contract is holdover agenda item from November/December 2025. Council requests presentation materials and projected timeline shared.

political_signal.description: Committee chair explicitly noting council hesitancy about another major expenditure; demands benchmarks in contract before fund disbursement
